title: The nature, distribution, and origin of gas hydrate in the Chile Triple Junction region
abstract: A bottom simulating reflector (BSR) is regionally distributed throughout the Chile Triple Junction region. ODP Leg 141 downhole temperature and logging data suggest the seismic BSR is generated by low seismic velocities associated with a few percent free gas in a ~10 m thick zone just beneath the hydrate-bearing zone. Low total organic carbon contents argue against significant in situ biogenic methanogenesis.

location.description: Chile Triple Junction region (~46°S); ODP Leg 141 drill sites in the accretionary prism at the Chile Ridge subduction zone

seismic_lines.name: ODP Leg 141 site survey MCS profiles — Chile Triple Junction

acquisition.vessel: R/V Conrad (pre-ODP site survey) and ODP Leg 141 JOIDES Resolution

data.name: ODP Leg 141 Site Survey MCS Profiles — Chile Triple Junction ~46°S
data.description: Pre-ODP site survey MCS reflection profiles (R/V Conrad) and drill-based downhole logging from ODP Leg 141 at the Chile Triple Junction; BSR regionally distributed throughout the region above gas hydrate zone. [R/V Conrad RC2901 (Lamont, early 1990s) — no confirmed public data portal found]

data.name: ODP Leg 141 Downhole Temperature and Velocity Logs — Chile Triple Junction
data.description: ODP Leg 141 downhole temperature and logging data used to constrain gas hydrate stability zone; indicates BSR generated by ~few percent free gas in a 10m zone below hydrate

_data_note: ODP Leg 141 site survey data from R/V Conrad (RC2901 cruises) accessible via MGDS/ODP TAMU. ODP downhole data at ODP TAMU.

processing.summary: Pre-ODP site survey MCS reflection profiles from the Chile Triple Junction region (~46°S) were analyzed for BSR distribution. ODP Leg 141 (JOIDES Resolution) downhole temperature measurements and velocity logs from drill cores constrain the gas hydrate stability zone. The BSR is regionally distributed and generated by a ~10m free gas zone (~few percent) immediately below the hydrate-bearing sediments. Low TOC argues against significant in situ methanogenesis.
workflow: 1. MCS reflection profiling and BSR mapping
4. Integration of BSR depth with thermal gradient
5. Geochemical analysis of cores for TOC
processing.notes: Integration of marine seismic data with ODP drilling results; ODP Leg 141 drill sites at ~46°S

analysis_notes: EPSL paper; behind paywall. ODP Leg 141 data publicly available via ODP TAMU. Site survey R/V Conrad data accessible via MGDS.
